section.title: The Queen of Sheba and Solomon's Death
section.sectionRationale: A foreign queen travels a great distance to test Solomon's famous wisdom with hard questions, and what she finds leaves her breathless, taking her breath away with wonder at his wisdom, wealth, and the ordered devotion of his household and his worship. The chapter then surveys Solomon's staggering wealth, his golden throne, his fleets, and his fame among all the kings of the earth, before closing simply with his death after forty years on the throne.
section.thesis: The queen of Sheba's confession that the report of Solomon's wisdom did not tell even half the truth, followed by a summary of unmatched wealth and honor, and then the plain, sobering notice of Solomon's death, together show that even the greatest of Israel's kings, blessed beyond any other, still could not escape the common end of all flesh, pointing forward to a King greater than Solomon whose reign will never end.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the queen of Sheba's visit and confession, verses 1 to 12; Solomon's wealth, throne, and fame, verses 13 to 28; and the closing summary and death of Solomon, verses 29 to 31.

units.label: The queen of Sheba's visit and confession
units.note: not half the greatness was told to me

verses.text: Now when the queen of Sheba heard about the fame of Solomon, she came to test him with difficult questions. She arrived in Jerusalem with a very large caravan, with camels bearing spices, gold in abundance, and precious stones. And she came to Solomon and spoke with him about all that was on her mind.
And Solomon answered all her questions; nothing was too difficult for him to explain.
When the queen of Sheba saw the {{chokmah|wisdom}} of Solomon, the palace he had built,

the food at his table, the seating of his servants, the service and attire of his attendants, the attire of his cupbearers, and the burnt offerings he presented at the house of the LORD, it took her breath away.
She said to the king, "The report I heard in my own country about your words and wisdom is true.
But I did not believe the reports until I came and saw with my own eyes. Indeed, not half of the greatness of your wisdom was told to me. You have far exceeded the report I heard.
How blessed are your men! How blessed are these servants of yours who stand continually before you and hear your wisdom!

Blessed be the LORD your God, who has delighted in you to set you on His throne to be king for the LORD your God. Because your God loved Israel enough to establish them forever, He has made you king over them to carry out {{mishpat|justice}} and righteousness."
Then she gave the king 120 talents of gold, a great quantity of spices, and precious stones. There had never been such spices as those the queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.
The servants of Hiram and of Solomon who brought gold from Ophir also brought algum wood and precious stones.

The king made the algum wood into steps for the house of the LORD and for the king's palace, and into lyres and harps for the singers. Never before had anything like them been seen in the land of Judah.
King Solomon gave the queen of Sheba all she desired, whatever she asked, far more than she had brought the king. Then she left and returned to her own country, along with her servants.
The weight of gold that came to Solomon each year was 666 talents,
not including the revenue from the merchants and traders. And all the Arabian kings and governors of the land also brought gold and silver to Solomon.
King Solomon made two hundred large shields of hammered gold; six hundred shekels of hammered gold went into each shield.
He also made three hundred small shields of hammered gold; three hundred shekels of gold went into each shield. And the king put them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.
Additionally, the king made a great throne of ivory and overlaid it with pure gold.
The throne had six steps, and a footstool of gold was attached to it. There were armrests on both sides of the seat, with a lion standing beside each armrest.
Twelve lions stood on the six steps, one at either end of each step. Nothing like this had ever been made for any kingdom.
All King Solomon's drinking cups were gold, and all the utensils of the House of the Forest of Lebanon were pure gold. There was no silver, because it was accounted as nothing in the days of Solomon.
For the king had the ships of Tarshish that went with Hiram's servants, and once every three years the ships of Tarshish would arrive bearing gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.
So King Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.
All the kings of the earth sought an audience with Solomon to hear the wisdom that God had put in his heart.
Year after year, each visitor would bring his tribute: articles of silver and gold, clothing, weapons, spices, horses, and mules.
Solomon had 4,000 stalls for horses and chariots, and 12,000 horses, which he stationed in the chariot cities and also with him in Jerusalem.
He reigned over all the kings from the Euphrates to the land of the Philistines, as far as the border of Egypt.
The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones, and cedar as abundant as sycamore in the foothills.
Solomon's horses were imported from Egypt and from all the lands.

As for the rest of the acts of Solomon, from beginning to end, are they not written in the Records of Nathan the Prophet, in the Prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, and in the Visions of Iddo the Seer concerning Jeroboam son of Nebat?
Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel forty years.
And Solomon {{shakab|rested}} with his fathers and was buried in the city of his father David. And his son Rehoboam reigned in his place.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom. It is Solomon's chokmah, first requested at Gibeon in chapter 1, that draws the queen of Sheba across a great distance, and it is this same wisdom, along with the ordered devotion she observes in his household and worship, that leaves her breathless with astonishment.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, right ruling. The queen recognizes that God made Solomon king specifically to carry out mishpat and righteousness, naming the very purpose for which wisdom was granted in the first place, back in chapter 1, to govern God's people well.

shakab.gloss: To lie down, to rest. The chapter's closing note that Solomon shakab, rested, with his fathers is the common, sober biblical idiom for death, a plain reminder that even the wisest and wealthiest king Israel ever knew still lay down at last among mortal men.

basic.context: The queen of Sheba, hearing of Solomon's fame, travels a great distance with a vast caravan of spices, gold, and precious stones to test him with hard questions. What she sees, his wisdom, his palace, his ordered household, and his worship at the temple, leaves her breathless, and she confesses that the reports she had heard did not even tell half the truth. The chapter then catalogs Solomon's staggering wealth, his golden throne with its twelve lions, his fleets bringing exotic treasures every three years, and his fame among all the kings of the earth, before closing simply and soberly: after forty years, Solomon rested with his fathers and died.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific royal visit and a summary of Solomon's reign; it is not a promise that wisdom automatically produces limitless wealth for every believer today. What is theologically significant is the juxtaposition the chapter creates: unmatched wisdom and wealth, praised even by a foreign queen who recognizes God's hand in it, followed immediately by the plain fact of death, showing that no earthly splendor, however God given, escapes mortality.
basic.originalLanguages: It is Solomon's chokmah, wisdom, that draws the queen across such a distance, and she recognizes that God gave it to him precisely so he could carry out mishpat, justice, and righteousness for his people. The chapter's final word on Solomon is that he shakab, rested, with his fathers, the Bible's common, unadorned way of naming death, even for the greatest of kings.
basic.typology: Jesus Himself points to this chapter, declaring that something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42, since the queen of Sheba traveled far to hear wisdom that would one day pale before the wisdom and person of Christ, freely available to all who seek Him. Solomon's wealth, wisdom, and fame among all the kings of the earth anticipate the greater King before whom every nation will one day bow, and whose reign, unlike Solomon's forty years, will never end.
basic.christ: Jesus explicitly invokes this queen, saying the Queen of the South will rise up in judgment and condemn this generation, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom, and now something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42. Solomon's wealth and honor, unmatched among earthly kings, are a shadow of the greater glory and kingdom that belong to Christ, the true Son of David whose throne, unlike Solomon's ivory and gold one, will be established forever, Luke 1:32-33.
basic.god: God delights to bless obedient wisdom generously, so much that even foreign rulers recognize His hand and bless Him for it. Yet God's Word does not shy away from recording, with plain honesty, that even Solomon, the most blessed king Israel ever had, still died like every other man, a sober reminder that all human glory is temporary, and lasting hope must rest in the God who reigns beyond any single king's lifetime.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 10 gives the parallel account of the queen of Sheba's visit and Solomon's wealth. Matthew 12:42 records Jesus declaring that something, and someone, greater than Solomon is here. Luke 1:32-33 promises that Christ's throne and kingdom will have no end, unlike Solomon's forty year reign. And Ecclesiastes, traditionally linked to Solomon's own reflection late in life, wrestles honestly with the fleeting nature of even the greatest earthly achievement.
basic.oneStory: The queen of Sheba's astonished confession and the catalog of Solomon's unmatched wealth mark the high point of Israel's earthly kingdom, yet the chapter's final, understated sentence, Solomon rested with his fathers and died, quietly insists that no earthly splendor lasts forever. The whole scene points forward to Christ, the true and greater Son of David, before whom every nation will one day marvel, and whose throne, unlike any human king's, will never come to an end.

context.literary: The chapter moves from a specific, personal encounter, the queen's visit and confession, verses 1 to 12, to an expansive, almost hymn like catalog of wealth and fame, verses 13 to 28, before ending abruptly with the barest possible notice of death, verses 29 to 31, a literary structure that lets the reader feel the full weight of Solomon's glory before the plain fact of mortality closes the account.
context.historical: Sheba is generally identified with the ancient kingdom of Saba in southwest Arabia, a major hub of the spice and incense trade, and the queen's visit reflects real diplomatic and commercial contact between Israel and Arabian trading powers at the height of the united monarchy. The gold, ivory, and exotic imports like apes and peacocks arriving by ship every three years reflect wide ranging maritime trade networks reaching well beyond the Mediterranean.
context.canonical: This chapter closely parallels 1 Kings 10:1-29 and 1 Kings 11:41-43, but the Chronicler, consistent with his selective focus, omits the account of Solomon's foreign wives and idolatry recorded in 1 Kings 11:1-40, choosing to close his portrait of Solomon on the high note of wisdom, wealth, and fame rather than the moral decline Kings also records, before the sober fact of death still closes the book on even this idealized picture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Solomon's reign at its most glorious height, confirmed even by an outside witness, so that the returned exiles could see what faithful, wisdom shaped kingship once achieved, while the closing notice of death reminds them, and every reader, that hope cannot ultimately rest in any human king, however blessed, but must look beyond him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical visit and summarizes a particular king's wealth; it does not prescribe that wisdom always yields such riches, nor does it promise every faithful believer comparable earthly splendor. What is enduring is the recognition, voiced even by a foreign queen, that true wisdom and just rule are gifts of God meant to bless His people, and the sober reminder that all such earthly glory eventually ends in death.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler omit Solomon's later apostasy, recorded in 1 Kings 11, from his account?

views.label: A deliberate idealized portrait for a struggling community
views.case: Writing for exiles without a king or a full temple, the Chronicler consistently emphasizes positive models of faithfulness; omitting Solomon's decline lets his account of Solomon's reign stand as an encouraging pattern of wisdom and temple centered worship rather than a cautionary tale, which the Chronicler reserves for other kings.

views.label: A theological rather than a comprehensive historical purpose
views.case: The Chronicler's stated sources, verse 29, indicate a fuller record existed elsewhere; his own selection is deliberately theological, tracing the temple and covenant themes rather than attempting an exhaustive biography, so the omission reflects purpose rather than ignorance or concealment.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the omission is intentional and consistent with the Chronicler's larger purpose, using Solomon's reign to model wisdom, worship, and the blessings that flow from seeking God, while the plain fact of Solomon's death still closes the account with appropriate sobriety.

study.originalLanguages: The queen of Sheba comes because of Solomon's chokmah, wisdom, and having seen it firsthand she recognizes that God gave it to him precisely to carry out mishpat, justice, and righteousness for His people. Yet the chapter's final word is unadorned: Solomon shakab, rested, with his fathers, the same simple verb Scripture uses again and again for death, refusing to let even the most glorious king escape the common end of all flesh.

typology.type: The queen of Sheba traveling far to test Solomon's wisdom
typology.fulfillment: The one greater than Solomon, Christ, whose wisdom the Queen of the South's journey anticipates and whom all nations are invited to seek, Matthew 12:42.

typology.type: Solomon's unmatched wealth, throne, and fame among all the kings of the earth
typology.fulfillment: Christ's everlasting throne and kingdom, before which every nation will one day bow, Luke 1:32-33, Philippians 2:9-11.

study.christ: Jesus explicitly points back to this chapter, declaring that the Queen of the South will rise up in judgment against an unbelieving generation, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42. Solomon's staggering wealth and honor, unmatched among the kings of the earth, are only a shadow of the greater glory belonging to Christ, the true Son of David, whose throne, unlike Solomon's ivory seat overlaid with gold, will be established forever and will never end, Luke 1:32-33.
study.god: God delights to bless wisdom used rightly, so generously that even a foreign queen recognizes His hand and blesses Him for it. Yet Scripture does not flinch from recording plainly that even Solomon, the most blessed king Israel ever knew, still died like every other man, teaching that lasting hope cannot rest in any human king's wealth or wisdom but only in the God who reigns beyond every human lifetime.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of the queen of Sheba's visit and Solomon's wealth.
Records Solomon's later apostasy and death, material the Chronicler notably omits from his own account.
Jesus declares that something greater than Solomon is here, invoking the queen of Sheba's journey.
Christ's throne and kingdom, unlike Solomon's, will have no end.

study.oneStory: The queen of Sheba's astonished confession and the sweeping catalog of Solomon's wealth mark the summit of Israel's earthly kingdom, yet the chapter's final, unadorned sentence, Solomon rested with his fathers and died, quietly insists that no human king's glory lasts forever. This whole scene points forward to Christ, the true and greater Son of David, before whom every nation will one day marvel, and whose everlasting throne no death can ever end.

quiz.q: Why did the queen of Sheba travel to Jerusalem?

options.text: To test Solomon with hard questions after hearing of his fame and wisdom.
options.note: Verse 1 states this explicitly as the purpose of her journey.

options.text: To form a military alliance against a common enemy.
options.note: The text presents her visit as a quest to test Solomon's wisdom, not a military alliance.

options.text: To demand tribute from Solomon.
options.note: She brought lavish gifts to Solomon rather than demanding tribute from him, verse 9.

options.text: The chapter gives no reason for her visit.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly states her reason for coming.

quiz.q: What was the queen of Sheba's response after seeing Solomon's wisdom and household firsthand?

options.text: She confessed that not even half the greatness of his wisdom had been told to her, and she blessed the LORD for setting Solomon on the throne to carry out justice and righteousness.
options.note: Verses 6-8 record this full confession.

options.text: She dismissed the reports as exaggerated and unimpressive.
options.note: The opposite occurred; she found the reality exceeded the reports, not fell short of them.

options.text: She demanded that Solomon abandon the worship of the LORD.
options.note: She instead blessed the LORD, Solomon's God, verse 8.

options.text: The chapter records no reaction from her at all.
options.note: Verses 5-8 record an extensive, detailed reaction and confession.

quiz.q: What details describe the scale of Solomon's wealth in this chapter?

options.text: 666 talents of gold arrived each year, silver was as common as stones, and his throne of ivory and gold was unmatched by any kingdom.
options.note: Verses 13, 15-20, and 27 all describe this staggering wealth in detail.

options.text: Solomon lived modestly, avoiding excessive wealth or display.
options.note: The chapter describes the opposite, an unprecedented scale of wealth and display.

options.text: Solomon's wealth came entirely from military conquest.
options.note: The wealth described comes from trade, tribute, and gifts, not conquest, as detailed in verses 9, 14, and 21-24.

options.text: The chapter contains no description of Solomon's wealth.
options.note: Verses 13-28 are an extended catalog of Solomon's wealth.

options.text: With the simple, sober notice that after forty years reigning in Jerusalem, Solomon rested with his fathers and died, and his son Rehoboam succeeded him.
options.note: Verses 29-31 record this plain closing statement.

options.text: With a lengthy account of a national celebration honoring Solomon's achievements.
options.note: The chapter's ending is notably brief and sober, not an extended celebration.

options.text: With a description of Solomon's temple being destroyed.
options.note: The temple's destruction comes centuries later, in 2 Chronicles 36, not at the end of this chapter.

options.text: The chapter never mentions Solomon's death.
options.note: Verse 31 explicitly records Solomon's death and burial.

quiz.q: How does Jesus later refer to this chapter's account of the queen of Sheba?

options.text: He says the Queen of the South will rise up in judgment against an unbelieving generation, since she came from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom, and something greater than Solomon is here.
options.note: Matthew 12:42 records Jesus making this exact connection.

options.text: Jesus never references the queen of Sheba or Solomon's wisdom anywhere in the Gospels.
options.note: Matthew 12:42 explicitly references her, directly connecting this chapter to Jesus' own teaching.

options.text: Jesus criticizes the queen of Sheba for her lack of faith.
options.note: Jesus commends her diligence in seeking wisdom, using her as a positive example against an unbelieving generation, not as a criticism of her.

options.text: Jesus claims Solomon's wisdom was never real.
options.note: Jesus affirms Solomon's wisdom while declaring that something even greater than it is now present in Himself.

questions: The queen of Sheba traveled a great distance to seek wisdom firsthand rather than relying only on reports. What truth about God have you only heard secondhand that you need to seek and test for yourself?
She confessed that the reality exceeded everything she had been told. When has your own experience of God's goodness exceeded what you expected or had been told?
Solomon's wealth was staggering, yet the chapter ends simply with his death. What does this teach you about where to place your ultimate hope, wealth and achievement, or something else?
Jesus said something greater than Solomon is here. How does knowing Christ's wisdom and kingdom surpass even Solomon's glory change what you are seeking most eagerly in your own life right now?
Solomon's reign, however glorious, still ended after forty years. What legacy do you want to leave that will outlast your own lifetime?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that the Septuagint and Syriac, matching 1 Kings 10:5, read and his burnt offerings that he presented, while the Hebrew Masoretic Text reads and his stairway by which he went up, a textual variant reflected in the translation choice.

Tarshish is generally understood as a distant western port, possibly in the western Mediterranean, and ships of Tarshish came to describe large, ocean going trading vessels generally, not necessarily ships bound specifically for that port.

The Chronicler cites three named prophetic sources, Nathan, Ahijah, and Iddo, for further records of Solomon's reign, none of which have survived as independent texts, distinct from the biblical books that bear similar prophetic names.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 9:1-31. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas.
